amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: Transcription elongation in E. coli involves RNA polymerase (RNAP) processively synthesizing RNA from the DNA template at ~40-50 nucleotides per second. The elongation complex maintains a transcription bubble of ~8-9 bp of unwound DNA and a ~9 bp RNA-DNA hybrid. RNAP exhibits intrinsic pausing at specific sequences, allowing time for regulatory factors to act. The process includes proofreading through backtracking and cleavage by GreA/GreB factors, antitermination by factors like NusA/NusG that suppress pausing, and coordination with coupling factors like NusG that link transcription to translation. The elongation complex must navigate DNA topology changes, deal with DNA-binding proteins, and respond to regulatory signals that modulate RNAP speed. This flowchart illustrates the complete elongation cycle including NTP addition, translocation, pausing mechanisms, proofreading, and antitermination pathways.
mermaid:
graph TD
    Start([Transcription Elongation Cycle]) --> A1[RNAP elongation complex on DNA]
    A1 --> A2["Transcription bubble: 8-9 bp unwound DNA"]
    A2 --> A3["RNA-DNA hybrid: 9 bp in RNAP active site"]
    A3 --> A4[RNA 3' end positioned in active site]
    
    A4 --> A5{NTP substrate available?}
    A5 -->|Yes - correct NTP| A6[NTP binds in i+1 site]
    A5 -->|Incorrect NTP| A7[Weak binding - rapid dissociation]
    A5 -->|No NTP| Wait1[RNAP pauses waiting for NTP]
    A7 --> A5
    Wait1 --> A5
    
    A6 --> A8[Template base pairs with incoming NTP]
    A8 --> A9[Trigger loop closes - catalytically active state]
    
    A9 --> A10[Two-metal-ion catalysis in active site]
    A10 --> A11[Phosphodiester bond formed]
    A11 --> A12[RNA extended by one nucleotide]
    A12 --> A13[Pyrophosphate PPi released]
    
    A13 --> A14[Trigger loop opens - post-catalytic state]
    
    A14 --> A15{Translocation successful?}
    A15 -->|Yes| A16[RNAP translocates forward 1 bp]
    A15 -->|Backtrack| A17[RNAP backtracks - 3' end leaves active site]
    
    A17 --> A18{GreA or GreB present?}
    A18 -->|Yes| A19[GreA/GreB binds secondary channel]
    A18 -->|No| A20[Backtracked complex arrested]
    
    A19 --> A21[GreA/GreB activates RNAP cleavage activity]
    A21 --> A22[/RNA 3' end cleaved - 2-3 nt removed GreA or 10+ nt GreB/]
    A22 --> A23[New 3' end repositioned in active site]
    A23 --> A16
    
    A16 --> A24[DNA translocated through RNAP]
    A24 --> A25[Downstream DNA unwound into bubble]
    A25 --> A26[Upstream DNA rewound behind RNAP]
    A26 --> A27[RNA-DNA hybrid slides forward maintaining 9 bp]
    
    A27 --> A28{Pause signal encountered?}
    A28 -->|No pause signal| A29[Normal elongation continues]
    A28 -->|Hairpin pause| A30[RNA hairpin forms in exit channel]
    A28 -->|Elemental pause| A31[Sequence-specific pause - weak RNA-DNA hybrid]
    A28 -->|Backtrack pause| A32[His-pause or other backtrack signal]
    
    A30 --> A33[Hairpin stabilizes paused state]
    A31 --> A34[Weak hybrid allows fraying]
    A32 --> A17
    
    A33 --> A35{Antitermination factors present?}
    A34 --> A35
    
    A35 -->|NusA present| A36[NusA binds RNAP and RNA]
    A35 -->|NusG present| A37[NusG binds RNAP clamp]
    A35 -->|No factors| A38[Extended pause]
    
    A36 --> A39[NusA can stabilize OR suppress pause depending on context]
    A37 --> A40[NusG suppresses pausing - processivity factor]
    
    A39 --> A41{Which NusA function?}
    A41 -->|Pause stabilization| A38
    A41 -->|Pause suppression| A42[Pause released]
    
    A40 --> A42
    A42 --> A29
    
    A29 --> A43{Termination signal encountered?}
    A43 -->|No| A44[Continue elongation cycle]
    A43 -->|Intrinsic terminator| A45[/Rho-independent termination pathway/]
    A43 -->|Rho-dependent| A46[/Rho-dependent termination pathway/]
    
    A44 --> A4
    
    A38 --> A47{Rho helicase present?}
    A47 -->|Yes - if rut site exposed| A48[Rho binds rut site on nascent RNA]
    A47 -->|No or rut covered by ribosome| A49[/Pause eventually releases or terminates/]
    
    A48 --> A50[Rho translocates 5' to 3' on RNA]
    A50 --> A51[Rho catches up to paused RNAP]
    A51 --> A46
    
    A45 --> End1[/(Intrinsic Termination)/]
    A46 --> End2[/(Rho-Dependent Termination)/]
    A49 --> A52{Context-dependent outcome}
    A52 -->|Resume| A42
    A52 -->|Terminate| A45
    
    A20 --> A53[/Arrested complex may require Mfd or degradation/]
    A53 --> End3([Transcript aborted])

name: Transcription Elongation
notes: Transcription elongation is highly regulated despite being a processive process. Pausing serves multiple functions including allowing time for regulatory decisions, coordinating with translation, and preventing collisions with DNA-binding proteins. The interplay between GreA/GreB (which rescue backtracked complexes) and NusG (which suppresses pausing) determines overall elongation rate. In bacteria, transcription and translation are coupled through NusG-ribosome interactions, linking RNA synthesis directly to protein synthesis. This coupling is lost in eukaryotes due to nuclear compartmentalization.
scientificAccuracy: This flowchart is based on extensive structural, biochemical, and single-molecule studies of bacterial transcription elongation. All components (RNAP, elongation factors GreA/GreB, NusA, NusG, Rho), catalytic mechanisms, pausing sites, and regulatory mechanisms have been validated through crystallography and single-molecule tracking. The model represents current understanding of how RNAP balances speed, accuracy, and regulatory responsiveness during RNA synthesis.
